nodes.text: Scanning the card now, huh? You know, I've been coming here since before they put those readers in. Used to just give a name and they'd find you. Simpler times.

responses.text: Times change. Just a couple of questions.
Your records go back a long way here.
I want to ask about your prescription.

nodes.text: Fire away. Date of birth, address, all of it. I've got it memorized. Nothing better to do on a Tuesday.

responses.text: Everything checks out. You're all set.

nodes.text: Fifteen years this April. Dr. Ellison put me on the warfarin about seven years ago — the A-fib. I get the INR checked every few weeks, keep it steady. It's become a whole system.

responses.text: That's exactly the kind of history I'd expect to see. Everything looks right.
Just need your date of birth to confirm.

nodes.text: Warfarin, five milligrams, Dr. Ruth Ellison. She's been with me since before the A-fib diagnosis. Good doctor. Thorough. Makes me come in more than I'd like but that's the deal.

responses.text: Fill history looks continuous. That's what I'd expect for warfarin.

nodes.text: July seventeenth, nineteen fifty-two. I've had the same birthday for seventy-one years and it hasn't surprised me yet.

responses.text: That matches. You're all set, Carl.

nodes.text: Good. I'll be over here with my paper. Cold enough out there you could still get snow. I'm usually right about these things.
